does: This paper proposes CALYREX, a transformer architecture modification that uses cross-attention mechanisms to structurally separate system prompts from user inputs in large language models. The core contribution is inserting dedicated cross-attention layers between the system prompt and input tokens, with optimal placement determined to be in the final eighth of model layers through ablation studies. The method is evaluated on a 1.5B backbone model and scaled to 8B parameters, demonstrating improvements of 7.4% on instruction-following benchmarks (IFEval) and 16.3% on multi-turn instruction adherence. Additionally, CALYREX reduces the success rate of many-shot jailbreaking attacks by 13%, showing measurable gains in model robustness against prompt injection while maintaining equivalent parameter budgets and training data compared to baseline models.
connects: This work addresses a fundamental vulnerability in modern LLMs identified by recent security research on prompt injection and instruction erosion attacks. It builds on established transformer architecture literature, particularly cross-attention mechanisms previously used in sequence-to-sequence models and multimodal architectures. The paper engages with concurrent work on system prompt robustness and safety alignment, responding to the recognition that standard causal self-attention treats all tokens equivalently and cannot preferentially preserve system-level behavioral constraints. The mechanistic analysis draws on interpretability methods that probe learned representations in transformer layers, extending this approach to understand where instruction-following constraints naturally concentrate in deep networks.
enables: This architectural innovation opens directions for more robust instruction-following at scale, suggesting that structural modifications to attention patterns can improve safety properties beyond standard training approaches. The finding that larger models better utilize dedicated cross-attention pathways indicates potential for scaling this technique to frontier models where prompt injection risks are most acute. The work enables future research into other structural separations between different input categories, such as isolating safety guidelines or separating user queries from retrieved context. It also points toward developing theoretical frameworks explaining why late-layer processing is critical for behavioral constraints, potentially informing the design of additional architectural safeguards for alignment.
